figures.caption: Figure 1: Stochastic Burgers’ Equation results. For a representative test initial condition, the figure shows the terminal mean field, variance field, and pointwise marginal density. MNO recovers a nontrivial residual variance profile while remaining competitive with deterministic baselines on mean prediction.

Figure 10: Diagnostic V: uncertainty decomposition. The plotted bars compare an under-trained ensemble proxy for in-distribution/out-of-distribution epistemic variance with MNO’s predicted in-distribution aleatoric variance. The true aleatoric scalar is 2.36 × 10−4, while MNO predicts 2.47× 10−4. The ensemble proxy is qualitative, not calibrated.

Figure 11: 2D turbulent flow (Kolmogorov-forced, NS-style, 64× 64). The figure shows the MNO mean prediction and its variance field; numerical comparisons with all reported baselines are given in Table 10.

Figure 2: Rough volatility results: comparison of MNO against sequential stochastic baselines across varying Hurst parameters H . MNO remains accurate in the deeply rough regime while targeting terminal marginals rather than pathwise roughness recovery.

Figure 3: ϕ4 field theory benchmark (SPDEBench). MNO achieves W2 = 0.0055 while Neural SPDE obtains W2 = 0.6572. The figure shows the learned terminal mean, variance field, and a representative marginal density induced by MNO’s low-rank residual factor.

Figure 12: 2D resolution transfer. Models are trained at 64× 64 and evaluated zero-shot at 32× 32, 64 × 64, and 128 × 128. All spectral and CNN baselines degrade sharply at 128 × 128 because modes=16 maps to different physical wavenumbers when the spatial grid changes; MNO sits at parity with the FNO baseline at every tested resolution. Full mean-RMSE values are reported in Table 11.

Figure 9: Diagnostic IV: head-separation audit. Across the three synthetic cases the trained MNO recovers the correct head energies: drift-energy is large only when drift is present (cases A and C), and covariance-energy is large only when noise is present (cases B and C). The two heads are therefore separable in this controlled setting.
Table 4: Hyperparameters for MNO Case Studies (Standard Mode)
Figure 13: 2D Gray-Scott reaction-diffusion. The reported run gives mean RMSE 0.0054 for FNO and 0.0345 for MNO; all reported 2D field metrics are listed in Table 10.

Figure 8: Diagnostic III: resolution-transfer variance. MNO is trained at resolution 64 and evaluated zero-shot through resolution 256. The left panel compares the highest-resolution variance fields; the right panel compares Fourier energy spectra. At resolution 256, variance-field RMSE is 0.00167 for MNO vs. 0.00853 for Noise-FNO. The spectrum is reported qualitatively: MNO tracks the low-frequency variance energy more closely, while fine-scale discrepancies remain.

Figure 7: Diagnostic II: Martingale Mirror. Left: one-shot MNO conditioned on u0 matches the plotted terminal marginal density for fBm with H = 0.1. Center: autoregressive reuse produces a wider density; the variance grows closer to linearly than to t2H = t0.2. Right: AR paths have Ĥ ≈ 0.486, while ground-truth paths have Ĥ ≈ 0.101, showing that repeated Markovian stepping changes the temporal scaling.

Figure 4: 1D zero-shot super-resolution. MNO (trained at resolution 32) achieves stable or improving mean RMSE at resolutions 32/64/128, while Neural SPDE degrades monotonically. The variance row is reported as an uncertainty diagnostic rather than a headline superiority claim at every resolution.

Figure 5: Generative efficiency comparison on stochastic Burgers’. MNO predicts terminal moments in a single forward pass (7.0× 10−3 s). The diffusion baseline requires 2.1× 10−2 s at NFE= 25. MNO also achieves lower W2 (0.0081 vs. 0.1589) under a matched 60-second wall-clock training budget.
Table 5: 1D stochastic terminal-law benchmarks. Lower is better for all metrics.

Figure 6: Diagnostic I: residual centering and scaling. The reported diagnostic records (i) near-zero temporal mean, (ii) approximately linear variance growth in the Brownian diagnostic, and (iii) weak correlation between selected residual increments and past-state features. These measurements check terminal residual behavior; they do not establish full filtration-level martingality.
Table 7: Rough volatility terminal-marginal results across Hurst parameter H . Lower is better for all metrics.
Table 6: 1D zero-shot superresolution. Models are trained at resolution 32 and evaluated at the listed resolution. Lower is better.
